questions.question: What is the recommended first step in April Dunford's 5-component positioning framework?

options.text: Picking a Market Category
Identifying Target Customer Segments
Finding Competitive Alternatives
Listing Unique Attributes

questions.explanation: The framework starts with Competitive Alternatives because you cannot define what makes you unique or valuable until you know what the customer would do if you didn't exist.

questions.question: For most B2B startups, who or what is often their biggest 'competitor'?

options.text: Direct competitors with similar features
Market leaders like Salesforce or Microsoft
Spreadsheets or 'doing nothing'
Open-source alternatives

questions.explanation: Often the biggest hurdle is 'status quo.' Most founders lose deals to spreadsheets, manual processes, or the customer simply deciding that the problem isn't worth the effort to solve.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a 'Unique Attribute' and a 'Differentiated Value'?

options.text: Attributes are about the customer; Value is about the product
Attributes are specific features or facts; Value is the outcome those features enable for the customer
There is no difference; they are the same thing
Value is technical; Attributes are emotional

questions.explanation: Unique Attributes are functional facts (e.g., 'Real-time API'), while Differentiated Value is the benefit the customer gets from that fact (e.g., 'Never make decisions with stale data').

questions.question: In the framework, when should you decide on your 'Market Category'?

options.text: At the very beginning, to set the scope
Right after listing your unique attributes
At the end, as it provides the context for all other decisions
Only after you have your first 10 customers

questions.explanation: Market Category is the last rung of the ladder because it must provide the best context for your unique value to be seen as a necessity rather than a nice-to-have.

questions.question: What is the 'Target Customer Segment' specifically looking for in this framework?

options.text: Anyone who might possibly use your product
The group of people who care MOST about your uniquely differentiated value
The largest possible market size (TAM)
Investors and partners who can help you scale

questions.explanation: Your target segment should be narrow—the people whose specific pains are solved exactly by your unique value. Targeting 'everyone' dilutes your messaging.

questions.question: Apply April Dunford's framework to your own business. What is the single biggest 'Competitive Alternative' your customers use today (e.g., a spreadsheet, doing nothing, or a direct rival)? Why do they choose that over a specialized solution?
questions.promptForAI: Evaluate the student's understanding of competitive alternatives. Check if they identified a specific alternative and explained the rationale behind its current use. Provide feedback on how their unique attributes might counter this alternative.
